sentences.text: Well, 1935, in June--
'39-- I'm sorry-- I graduated, and I was graduated in flying colors.
So I was terribly, terribly happy.
Everybody was happy.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went on holidays with my mother and my sister and my brother.
And my father used to stay home and come to join us on-- for the Shabbat and Sunday.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was for two months, we used to go.
So I felt so relieved that, you know, I'll have-- I'll, I'll be able to see my friends and have a good time and read a lot.
I love reading.

And so about two weeks before the war, when my father was-- has joined us on the weekend, he said, there are rumors that the Germans are surrounding Poland, far, far from-- far, far away from the borders.
But something is going on, and maybe it would be wise to come, to come home earlier.
And so we came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 27th of August or something like that.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the-- one day, I got on the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And went-- I just had to meet some girls at the king's palace, the king's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wawel, in Cracow.
It's called Wawel, W-A-W-E-L.
That was the king's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the kings were there when Poland was a kingdom.
And it's very beautiful.
So we used to sort of meet there, and the Vistula River beneath, and it was very beautiful.
So I took the tram.
I was a lazy walker always.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And suddenly, the sirens-- we heard sirens.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the tram stopped, and we were told to get out.
So all the passengers started laughing.
They said, it's only an exercise.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a policeman came on the tram.
And he said, no.
It's not an exercise.
We are at war with Germany.
We were invaded this morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was 1st of September, and we still laughed it off.
We didn't want to get off the tram.
But they were quite [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adamant about it, that we have to get out.
So we did.
There were no shelters.
We had to just go into the first building there was that was the closest.
And it looked serious, no people in the streets, everybody off the street.
And, and inside, I hoped that I am wrong-- that I am right, I mean, that it's only [BACKGROUND NOISE] an exercise.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, it was serious.
And then I started worrying about my parents.
I wanted to be at home with my parents, with my brother, with my sister.
I didn't want to-- if it's war, then you don't want to be away from them.
And we were told that-- only when the sirens stopped, that-- luckily enough, I was about two stops from my home.
So I quickly got later on the tram and got home.
Well, it didn't take long, two days or three days.

I remember that on the radio, there was the rabbi of Cracow and the archbishop of Cracow saying prayers and assuring us, everybody, the residents of Cracow, that Cracow, no matter what, won't be touched by, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the war, and no building will be destroyed.
This stuck in my mind, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, they were right, of course.
Cracow, only one building got hit.
There is, there is a reason behind it, too, because Cracow is over 1,000 years old, and [?

Hitler ?] obviously wanted to preserve it because, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, of the antiques and-- Well, then suddenly, we heard on the 3rd of September, a German voice, language saying that they have reclaimed the German city of Cracow.
Cracow was never German.
I mean, Austro-Hungarian, but generally, it-- it was a genuine Polish territory.
And they warned not to leave homes and not to come to the windows.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we all kept together, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister and I and my mother.
But when we heard the boots, the marching soldiers going through, we just went.
And through the curtain, we looked, and we couldn't believe our eyes.
11 people were already hanging.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They hung 11 people.
Mixed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews and non-Jews.
It stretched from the corner, opposite our building, to the corner, to the next corner of the street.
That was, that was something we thought that we see in a dream.
It can't be real, but it was real.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was real, unfortunately.
Well, then the tragedy started.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next, they were allowed to queue up at the bakery for bread.
The queues were so long, and the Germans are-- were grabbing from the queue, taking people.
Nobody knew where they were, never coming back.
So it was terribly, terribly frightening.
And at the same time, they passed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a law, I suppose-- it was hanging on the walls everywhere-- that the doors of our flats should be forever open, day and night.
And that's the first experience, the first bitter experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were coming into our flat or unit or whatever you prefer to call it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First they threw out whatever they could of the wardrobes everywhere.
My mother, at that time, wasn't well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since the minute she saw the people hanging there, she, she broke down, and, and she just couldn't accept it.
And she said then and there, I'm not going to live through that.
Is something happening to me?
So we called a Jewish doctor, because my uncle already fled to the Russian side.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they didn't know what it was.
Anyhow, she was getting worse from hour to hour.
She was very, very sick.
They were giving her injections of morphine.
She had pains all over.
So I was petrified.
I was-- on both fronts, on my home front, and, and the Nazis around me.
And they were coming and going, and into the flat.
They ripped up the parquet floors, looking for valuables.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And they went into the bedroom, saw my mother, and they told her to get up.
But she was dozing after the morphine, and I said, she can't.
She's sick.
They threw her off her bed.
And with their boots, they were trapping on her, and, and just to death.
But I didn't realize.
I haven't seen death before.
So I didn't realize that she's dead.
Pardon?
Yes.
And I begged them.
I cried.
I begged.
And each time I opened my mouth, I was getting a slap in the face, a slap in the head, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the back, and every possible place.
Meanwhile, I heard that they are screaming at my father and my brother.
They were told-- they were telling my father to do push-ups and to my brother.
They could hardly breathe already.
And my father was a, a Jew with a beard, and they wanted to rip out his beard.
So because it was happening next to the desk, I grabbed the scissors, and I sort of cut it off so that my father would still have the feeling that he has a beard.
But the push-ups, all the time.
And then, it's not, not pleasant to-- sorry.
It's not pleasant to talk about it.
There are young people here.
But they put their hands into the vaginas of my sister and myself, looking for valuables.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was bleeding.
My brother was bleeding from the push-ups, from the kicks they were getting with the boots.
And I hoped that my mother there is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] [SOBBING] [DRINKS] And they left.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the kitchen to grab a wet-- to sponge my father's face and my brother's and my little sister's.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and German-- these were SS men.
And the German officer came in.
He was, well, maybe 55, maybe 60 or so.
But he, he was from the Wehrmacht.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't say a word.
We couldn't pull ourselves together from that experience.
And he says, please, don't be afraid of me.
I am the older generation.
I am only a soldier.
And he sat down.
He even asked, may I sit down?
And I said, please do.
You seem pretty human.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father said to him, never, ever would I imagine that the nation that produced [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Schiller and, and, Beethoven and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the great people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could do something like that.
It's absolutely inhuman.
And he said, I have to not only agree with you, but I want you to understand my pain.
I have a son in the SS.
I don't want to know him.
I don't want to see him, and I don't want-- I hope he never comes back.
So when he left, I made a cup of tea for him.
No.
If he-- even if he would, I, I wouldn't have remembered.
And he talked to my father, and my father was very knowledgeable in German literature.
So-- and they talked.
And my father's nose was, was running with blood.
And I begged my father to keep his head back and the cold sponge on.
Anyhow, he left.
And he said, I'm just as disgusted with the regime as you are.
And I said, why-- how come you trust us and you are telling us all about it?
One has to talk to someone sometimes, he said.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he left.

And then next day, in the morning, we were told all the silver-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the tenants', not only us-- not the Christians, the Jews-- all the silver to be taken down to the-- on the truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, I started going out with the pieces.
And my parents had an enormous amount of antique silver and such silver, other silver.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You had to have for Pesach.
You had to have [POLISH], and everything had to be for at least 24 people and trays and, and, and teapots and whatnot, everything of sterling silver.
So I was afraid that I may-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it may get damaged.
So I was running down with my sister with the, the items.
And they smacked us and said, no.
You throw it from the balcony, down to the truck.
It was heartbreaking.
But these were things.
So my father said, don't-- said, don't worry about things.
Just us.
My mother was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] She still had a burial in Plaszow, where the camp was built.
So there's no grave, because the, the [NON-ENGLISH], the tombstones, were used as-- taken off and used as, you know, to walk on in the German quarter, where they lived there.
Pardon?
After that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, we still kept-- we were starving, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because all you could have was bread on the black market.
Wherever there is a thing like that, the black market is flourishing.
So we managed to get some barley and this.
I was never cooking.
I didn't know how to cook.
My-- I was devastated by my mother's death.
Actually, I wanted to throw myself off the window.
I couldn't-- you know, it was just too much to take at once-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not even at once.
In the long run, it was too much.
And, and I started cooking the barley.
I thought, I am cooking a soup.
The spoon was standing in it.
I didn't know that the barley expands when you-- I put in too much.
Anyhow, this is not important.
And so I wouldn't let my father out by himself.
Wherever he had to go, I went with him to sort of protect him.
But how I could protect him, I don't know.
I just had the feeling I didn't want him to be alone.
Oh, yes, yes.
And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we lived like this with every day, new, new things, new rules coming up.
We were not to employ non-Jewish labor.
So the maid, of course, had, has gone three days after the war came.
Non-Jewish doctors were not to attend to Jewish patients.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although I must say, when my mother wasn't feeling well, I did manage to get a non-Jewish doctor.
He came in the dark-- before the curfew, but in the dark.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he, he, he just couldn't do anything.
He did cut out a lump under her armpit.
And I had to take off the Star of David-- because that came into law immediately-- and went to the Institute of Pathology.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, well, it was cancer.
So anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived like this till we were taken to the ghetto.
We were given 24 hours.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.
I, I, I jumped something.
There was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something came out that we all have to register.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people above 50 or whatever-- I can't remember now-- had to leave Cracow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and go within the perimeter of 30 kilometers, past the perimeter of 30 kilometers from Cracow.
So obviously, it was my prime interest to set up my father somewhere.
And the rest of us had to get then what they called an ausweis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we got the ausweis.
What is the ausweis?
It's like an identity card.
We got it, and my father didn't.
But there was a little country town in which my father was born.
And I quickly went to this country town.
And because my great-grandparents, great-grandfather was the mayor of this town, I thought maybe someone will remember us.
And I went immediately to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it-- where everybody is registered.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found someone who remembered the name of the family-- a Polish, of course, a Polish man.
And I told him that I have to bring my father.
Could anybody [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] be able to supply just a room for him?
And he said, oh, for Mr.
Florenz's great-grandson.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I didn't know that he was so remembered and famous.
I didn't know about it, but it came handy.
And he said, you bring your father.
But you can't, you couldn't-- we have learned not to trust, you see?
So I said, look, I am a young person.
I was taught to trust people.
However, under the circumstances, where we are not very popular in the regime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure I can trust you.
I'll bring my father, but I can assure you that if we survive the war, you will be compensated for it.
But at the moment, there's nothing I can offer to you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said I've got some gold coins which I can give you, but I think it would be only honest if I give it to you after I have seen what happens to my father.
I brought my father, and he took him to some Polish family.
And in, in the attic, they put my father.
Wintertime, every Friday afternoon, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no.
That wasn't it yet.
First, they, they told us that within 24 hours-- so I have located my father.
And I took my little sister there so that he wouldn't be lonely.
And then I had to take care of the new legislation that within 24 hours, we have to go to the ghetto.
Otherwise, if we are found outside the ghetto, we will be shot.
In 1941, in March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I asked my brother to come with me, because there were so many thousands of people.
You could hardly get in there to-- You, you had to get a, a room allocated.
You had, had to be allocated a room, or rooms, or whatever to be able to go there.
And then, of course, you had a problem, how to get the things there, because everybody wanted a, a horse and a buggy.
So it was, it was absolutely-- I said to my brother, you look for a horse and a buggy.
And I will go and look-- try to get in to get into the ghetto and an accommodation.
But my brother, by nature, was very pessimistic.
He gave up then and there and said, I am not interested.
We are not going to live through it.
They may shoot me right, right here.
I won't fight them.
I am a nobody towards a big army like that.
And I said, well, I am not giving up.
So I had to be-- but we had a tenant who was-- who had a horse and a buggy.
Well, this was called a [POLISH], a [POLISH].
And he said-- I went to him, and they were owing us heaps of money for rent.
So I said, will you take-- all I want you to take is my father's library.
We'll break here for end of the tape.
